tweets.text:
Targeted enforcement under Tom Homan sounds smart on TV.
It’s actually one of the dumbest uses of manpower I’ve ever heard of.

Just laid it out on @MegynKellyShow 

Here’s a real-world system failure straight from CBP officers who lived it and then relayed to me as having happened today:

They’re running surveillance on a Colombian crew tied to a wave of residential burglaries in Los Angeles. Four officers, full day on the house. 

They watch the suspect’s three kids walk out. Then two other women roll up with four more kids and go inside. While they’re sitting there doing “precision” work, eight other illegals just stroll right past their position like it’s a parade.

End of shift?

One guy in custody. 

The other 17? Still here!

Four trained officers.
Hours of surveillance.
One arrest.

That’s what “targeted enforcement” actually looks like on the ground. 

It’s not strategy — it’s a jobs program for bureaucrats who want to look busy while the border starts to deteriorate and American families keep getting robbed.

You want results? You create fear. Mass operations. Roving patrols. Zero mixed signals.

Anything less is just expensive theater that tells every illegal in the country: “Relax, they’re only coming for the ‘bad ones’… eventually.”

The system is broken by design.

We’re not fixing it with teaspoons.

Trained to protect your ass, not kiss it.

That’s not a bumper sticker., folks. That’s the job description Border Patrol has lived for 102 years.

Not because I hate anyone, but because protecting your ass (and your kids’ and your neighbors’) is literally the kindest, smartest thing we can do.

Fake compassion gets people killed.

Tough love keeps them alive.

You already know which one actually works.

Who’s tired of the ass-kissing? 

Drop a 🔥 if you’re ready for real protection.

Folks, keep this in mind: 42% of the world  (thats 3.4 billion people!) still don’t have safely managed sanitation. No proper toilets, no plumbing, no waste treatment. 

That’s straight from the WHO/UNICEF 2025 report.

Despite these alarming, and filthy facts, our lawmakers choose to allow mass immigration from exactly those countries. 

If they can’t build or maintain basic toilet infrastructure where they came from, what makes us think they won’t recreate those same filthy conditions here?

Importing the problems people are supposedly fleeing isn’t compassion. It’s a recipe for turning our own country into what they left behind.

tweets.text:
Understanding Immigration - Using Gumballs https://t.co/NV82wWa4Zd via @YouTube

Your heart aches seeing that talented kid from Congo or families trapped in poverty overseas. You want to help. That instinct is good, but the gumballs don’t lie.

One gumball = 1 million people. 

America takes about a million immigrants a year. 

The world’s poor? 

Thousands of gumballs, and their numbers keep exploding.

We’ve tried the big experiment: massive immigration while straining our schools, hospitals, wages, housing, and trust. 

The result? A weaker, more divided America less able to help anyone with the root problems back home untouched.

True compassion isn’t importing a handful while the jar overflows. It’s fixing what doesn’t work over there (stable families, good governance, real opportunity) without wrecking what does work here.

We can be generous without being suicidal. 

The math deserves better policy than feelings.

What do you think?

tweets.text:
Ron DeSantis gets it.

While others chase clicks and headlines, he’s playing the long game — because America’s future is on the line.

They sold us the Hart-Celler Act in 1965: “Don’t worry, it won’t change our culture...it's largely symbolic.”

60 years later? 95% of our immigration comes from the Third World. That wasn’t a tweak. That was a total rewrite.

This is why hospital ERs are overcrowded, schools are strained, and the cost of living keeps skyrocketing. 

They promised one thing. Delivered the exact opposite.

This is why DeSantis stands out. He thinks like a real risk manager. He governs on stats, trends, and reality — not feelings. 

All issues are downstream of immigration, and he treats it like the upstream priority it is.

Basic risk management isn’t hate. It’s common sense.

Secure the borders. Protect the future for our kids. 

Prepare for the AI century instead of importing yesterday’s cheap labor model.

Florida gets this. America needs more of it.

What do you think?

tweets.text:
Most people hear “deport only the worst of the worst” and think, “Yeah, that sounds fair and targeted.”  That’s exactly why it’s such a perfect soundbite, it feels reasonable to people who don’t fully understand what we’re really up against.  

As I explained in Part 2 of my NPA podcast, it’s actually code for de facto amnesty for the other 100+ million illegal aliens already here. It also messages the next wave it will attract.  

Every single one of them is still crushing American wages, packing our ERs and classrooms, and turning sanctuary cities into magnets for more chaos.  

I didn’t read this in a think tank. I lived it for decades as a Border Patrol Commander-at-Large.  If you want the unfiltered truth from someone who was in the fight, not the media spin,  this 23-minute interview will completely rewire how you see the immigration debate.  

It’s not just about crime. It’s about whether America keeps working for Americans.  Watch it here. Then decide if “worst of the worst” still sounds like a plan… or a trap..... @NatPoliceAssociation
